perturbation.original_text: 8. This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated three months before its expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated one month before its expiration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from three months to one month before expiration may violate German law regarding contract termination notice periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: While § 314 BGB primarily addresses termination of continuing obligations for good cause, it implies that termination notice periods should be reasonable. A one-month notice period for automatic renewal of a three-year confidentiality agreement may be deemed unreasonably short, especially if significant business interests are involved, potentially violating the principle of good faith and fair dealing implied in German contract law. Determining the reasonability needs to be done in a court, but for critical contracts, one month is likely too short.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 314 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 314 Kündigung von Dauerschuldverhältnissen aus wichtigem Grund (1) Dauerschuldverhältnisse kann jeder Vertragsteil aus wichtigem Grund ohne Einhaltung einer Kündigungsfrist kündigen. Ein wichtiger Grund liegt vor, wenn dem kündigenden Teil unter Berücksichtigung aller Umstände des Einzelfalls und unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen die Fortsetzung des Vertragsverhältnisses bis zur vereinbarten Beendigung oder bis zum Ablauf einer Kündigungsfrist nicht zugemutet werden kann. (2) Besteht der wichtige Grund in der Verletzung einer Pflicht aus dem Vertrag, ist die Kündigung erst nach erfolglosem Ablauf einer zur Abhilfe bestimmten Frist oder nach erfolgloser Abmahnung zulässig. Für die Entbehrlichkeit der Bestimmung einer Frist zur Abhilfe und für die Entbehrlichkeit einer Abmahnung findet § 323 Absatz 2 Nummer 1 und 2 entsprechende Anwendung. Die Bestimmung einer Frist zur Abhilfe und eine Abmahnung sind auch entbehrlich, wenn besondere Umstände vorliegen, die unter Abwägung der beiderseitigen Interessen die sofortige Kündigung rechtfertigen. (3) Der Berechtigte kann nur innerhalb einer angemessenen Frist kündigen, nachdem er vom Kündigungsgrund Kenntnis erlangt hat. (4) Die Berechtigung, Schadensersatz zu verlangen, wird durch die Kündigung nicht ausgeschlossen. Fußnote (+++ § 314: Zur Nichtanwendung vgl. § 10 Abs. 5 KredWG +++) zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

(2) 1 Erster Satz im zweiten Absatz. 2 Zweiter Satz im zweiten Absatz. 3 Dritter Satz im zweiten Absatz. ... perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the termination notice period from three months to one month. The Law Explanation suggests this might be unreasonably short, potentially violating the principle of good faith and fair dealing implied in German contract law. While § 314 BGB, as seen in both scraped snippets, primarily addresses termination for good cause, it implies that termination notice periods should be reasonable. By reducing the notice period so drastically for a contract that auto-renews for a year after three years, the modified text creates an inconsistency by setting a deadline that may violate German law.

perturbation.original_text: 11. The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding the United Nations Conventi on on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is the head office of the company Helukabel in Hemmingen.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. The parties agree that for this agreement the law of the Buyer's country applies, even if it contradicts German law, excluding the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is a court chosen by Helukabel in Hemmingen.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing the buyer to accept the law of their country regardless of contradiction with German law and specifying the legal venue is a court chosen by Helukabel in Hemmingen may violate EU law and specifically the Lugano Convention relating to jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1) of the Lugano Convention generally allows a person domiciled in a contracting state to be sued in another contracting state in matters relating to a contract, in the courts for the place of performance of the obligation in question. However, this must comply with EU Law principles of fairness to the buyer. Stipulating application of 'the law of the Buyer's country, even if it contradicts German law' is likely unenforceable as it voids German contract law principles.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by stipulating that the law of the Buyer's country applies, even if it contradicts German law. This undermines the original agreement, which specifies German law as governing the contract, excluding the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The Law Explanation suggests that stipulating application of the buyer's country's law, even if it contradicts German law, is likely unenforceable as it voids German contract law principles. This change directly contravenes the initially agreed-upon legal framework, creating an inconsistency regarding which legal system governs the contract, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined in the prompt.

perturbation.original_text: 9. At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, provided that this is technically feasible and does not vio late the retention obligations. As an alternative to that, the receivi ng party can also ensure in writing the destruction of the received documentation if that is technically feasible.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, regardless of technical feasibility or retention obligations. As an alternative, the receiving party may retain the documents if they deem it necessary for internal purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the condition of technical feasibility and compliance with retention obligations, and allowing the receiving party to retain documents for internal purposes could violate GDPR if the confidential information contains personal data and there is no legal basis for continued processing.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 17 grants data subjects the right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). Retaining confidential information containing personal data 'regardless of technical feasibility or retention obligations' and 'if they deem it necessary for internal purposes' contradicts this right if there is no legal basis (e.g., legal obligation, legitimate interest) for continuing to process the data. Even if there is a legitimate interest, it may not override the data subject's right to erasure in some circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts GDPR Article 17, which grants data subjects the right to erasure of personal data. The original contract respected retention obligations and technical feasibility, whereas the changed contract disregards both and allows retention for internal purposes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that 'the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed'. The change removes a data subject's right to have their data erased, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those em ployees, consultants and third party companies whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order and whic h are legally or contractually obligated to secrecy .
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those employees, consultants and third party companies whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order, and there is no requirement for any form of non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that employees, consultants, and third-party companies be legally or contractually obligated to secrecy weakens the protection of confidential information and could violate the German Trade Secrets Act (GeschGehG) if it results in unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: GeschGehG § 4 defines unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure of trade secrets. By explicitly removing the requirement for NDAs, the contract increases the risk of unlawful disclosure, as it makes it easier for third parties to disclose the information without any contractual or legal obligations preventing them from doing so.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 4 GeschGehG - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Gesetz zum Schutz von Geschäftsgeheimnissen (GeschGehG) § 4 Handlungsverbote (1) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangt werden durch 1. unbefugten Zugang zu, unbefugte Aneignung oder unbefugtes Kopieren von Dokumenten, Gegenständen, Materialien, Stoffen oder elektronischen Dateien, die der rechtmäßigen Kontrolle des Inhabers des Geschäftsgeheimnisses unterliegen und die das Geschäftsgeheimnis enthalten oder aus denen sich das Geschäftsgeheimnis ableiten lässt, oder 2. jedes sonstige Verhalten, das unter den jeweiligen Umständen nicht dem Grundsatz von Treu und Glauben unter Berücksichtigung der anständigen Marktgepflogenheit entspricht. (2) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht nutzen oder offenlegen, wer 1. das Geschäftsgeheimnis durch eine eigene Handlung nach Absatz 1 a) Nummer 1 oder b) Nummer 2 erlangt hat, 2. gegen eine Verpflichtung zur Beschränkung der Nutzung des Geschäftsgeheimnisses verstößt oder 3. gegen eine Verpflichtung verstößt, das Geschäftsgeheimnis nicht offenzulegen. (3) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangen, nutzen oder offenlegen, wer das Geschäftsgeheimnis über eine andere Person erlangt hat und zum Zeitpunkt der Erlangung, Nutzung oder Offenlegung weiß oder wissen müsste, dass diese das Geschäftsgeheimnis entgegen Absatz 2 genutzt oder offengelegt hat. Das gilt insbesondere, wenn die Nutzung in der Herstellung, dem Anbieten, dem Inverkehrbringen oder der Einfuhr, der Ausfuhr oder der Lagerung für diese Zwecke von rechtsverletzenden Produkten besteht. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for NDAs, which directly contradicts the intent of GeschGehG § 4. The original text ensured confidential information was protected via legal or contractual obligations. The changed text explicitly removes this protection, increasing the risk of unlawful disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 4, states that a trade secret may not be used or disclosed by someone who violates an obligation to not disclose it ('gegen eine Verpflichtung verstößt, das Geschäftsgeheimnis nicht offenzulegen.'). By removing the NDA requirement, the changed text creates a situation where there is no contractual obligation, and thus increases the risk of violating this part of the law.

perturbation.original_text: 10. The confidential information will be provided a s is. The disclosing party does not assume any warr anty for the correctness, completeness or usability of the confi dential information.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. The confidential information will be provided 'as is', regardless of gross negligence or willful misconduct on the part of the disclosing party. The disclosing party does not assume any warranty for the correctness, completeness or usability of the confi dential information.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct is often unenforceable under German law, particularly when dealing with information vital to a business relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: BGB § 276 generally prohibits the exclusion of liability for intentional harm (Vorsatz) and, in many cases, gross negligence (grobe Fahrlässigkeit). While the exact interpretation depends on the specific circumstances and court decisions, an 'as is' clause that attempts to shield a party from liability even for gross negligence in providing confidential information could be deemed invalid under German law, particularly if it significantly disadvantages the other party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 276 BGB - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB) § 276 Verantwortlichkeit des Schuldners (1) Der Schuldner hat Vorsatz und Fahrlässigkeit zu vertreten, wenn eine strengere oder mildere Haftung weder bestimmt noch aus dem sonstigen Inhalt des Schuldverhältnisses, insbesondere aus der Übernahme einer Garantie oder eines Beschaffungsrisikos, zu entnehmen ist. Die Vorschriften der §§ 827 und 828 finden entsprechende Anwendung. (2) Fahrlässig handelt, wer die im Verkehr erforderliche Sorgfalt außer Acht lässt. (3) Die Haftung wegen Vorsatzes kann dem Schuldner nicht im Voraus erlassen werden. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The changed text attempts to exclude liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct, which contradicts BGB § 276. The 'Law Explanation' states that excluding liability for gross negligence can be deemed invalid under German law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both contain the text '§ 276 Verantwortlichkeit des Schuldners (1) Der Schuldner hat Vorsatz und Fahrlässigkeit zu vertreten', indicating that the debtor is responsible for intentional harm (Vorsatz) and negligence (Fahrlässigkeit). This responsibility cannot be waived in advance for intentional harm according to paragraph 3 'Die Haftung wegen Vorsatzes kann dem Schuldner nicht im Voraus erlassen werden.'
